verdicts.new_stem: A 24-day-old term male infant is brought to ED at 9 pm with a measured rectal temperature of 38.3 deg C two hours ago and reduced feeding (about 40 percent of usual volumes) for 12 hours. Birth was uncomplicated at 40 weeks, maternal group B Streptococcus status was negative, and his mother received no intrapartum antibiotics. He has been previously well, is exclusively breastfed, and there have been no rashes, vomiting, diarrhoea, cough or coryza. His 4-year-old sibling has a clear runny nose without fever. He is fully immunised for age (no scheduled vaccinations yet due). He looks pale, is consolable but quiet, and his parents specifically report he is not interacting as he normally does at this time of evening. The ED registrar has performed peripheral blood cultures, urinalysis (catheter), and bedside FBC and CRP, and is now planning antibiotic cover.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen, paired with timing of lumbar puncture, is most appropriate?

new_options.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy now; defer LP until CRP is back to decide whether it is needed
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone does not cover Listeria, which remains a real (though uncommon) pathogen in infants under 4 weeks; deferring LP to await CRP delays diagnosis of meningitis and is contrary to RCH CPG: Febrile child for under-4-week infants.

new_options.text: IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin now; defer LP until after antibiotics if the infant is too unwell to safely lie flat
new_options.rationale: This is the under-7-day neonatal sepsis regimen; for an infant 7 days to under 4 weeks, third-generation cephalosporin cover is added because of higher Gram-negative enteric and pneumococcal risk per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Lumbar puncture first, then IV benzylpenicillin plus IV gentamicin plus IV cefotaxime once CSF is obtained
new_options.rationale: For a febrile infant aged 7 days to under 4 weeks the RCH-recommended empirical regimen is benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime (Listeria, Gram-negatives, and CNS penetration for pneumococcus). LP is performed before antibiotics if the infant is stable; if unstable, antibiotics are given first and LP follows. This 24-day-old is haemodynamically reasonable (CRT 3 s without shock), so LP first is appropriate, per RCH CPG: Febrile child and RCH CPG: Sepsis.

new_options.text: Await CRP and FBC results before starting antibiotics; perform LP only if CRP is over 40 mg/L
new_options.rationale: Antibiotic-by-biomarker is unsafe in a febrile under-4-week infant; CRP cut-offs do not rule out invasive bacterial infection at this age, per RCH CPG: Febrile child.

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily plus admission for observation; reserve LP for clinical deterioration
new_options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for possible neonatal sepsis or meningitis; IV therapy and LP are mandatory in a febrile infant under 4 weeks, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

verdicts.new_stem: A previously well 18-month-old boy is brought to ED after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 2 minutes. He had been febrile since the morning with clear coryza and a tympanic temperature of 39.0 deg C. After the seizure he was drowsy for 15 minutes and then returned to baseline, drinking and interacting normally with his parents. There is a paternal history of childhood febrile seizures and he is fully immunised. On arrival he is alert and interactive with a temperature of 38.4 deg C, no neck stiffness, no rash, and no focal neurology. The ear examination shows a right bulging erythematous tympanic membrane. He has not had recent antibiotics and there is no head injury.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of investigation and management is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Non-contrast CT brain and admission for 24-hour observation; commence regular paracetamol
new_options.rationale: Routine neuroimaging is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with normal neurology and an identified febrile focus, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure.

new_options.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone because of the seizure during fever
new_options.rationale: LP is not indicated in a fully immunised over-12-month-old with a simple febrile seizure who has returned to baseline without meningism, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure. LP-before-treat reasoning is the anti-pattern tested here.

new_options.text: Prophylactic oral sodium valproate to prevent recurrence with neurology outpatient referral
new_options.rationale: Long-term anticonvulsants are not used after simple febrile seizures, even with positive family history, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure.

new_options.text: No investigations; treat the acute otitis media per eTG criteria, provide written first-aid advice, discharge home
new_options.rationale: This is a simple febrile seizure (single, generalised, under 15 minutes, full recovery, 6 months to 6 years) with an identified bacterial focus (right bulging TM in a child under 2 with severe symptoms meets eTG criteria for amoxicillin 30 mg/kg q8h for 5 days). No investigations are required. Reassurance and written first-aid advice are standard per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

new_options.text: Admit overnight for continuous EEG and reassess for discharge in the morning
new_options.rationale: EEG is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with full recovery, per RCH CPG: Febrile seizure.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old previously well boy presents on day 6 of fever. His mother reports daily fevers peaking at 39.5 deg C, unresponsive to alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, cracked red lips, a strawberry tongue, a polymorphous truncal rash, and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy with one node 1.8 cm. His hands and feet are red and swollen. He has no respiratory symptoms, no diarrhoea, and no household contacts with rash. He is fully immunised. Initial bloods show CRP 138 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, platelets 530 x10^9/L, ALT 78 U/L. Urine microscopy shows sterile pyuria. Echocardiography is being arranged.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of immediate therapy and aspirin dosing is most appropriate?

new_options.text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single infusion plus high-dose aspirin (30-50 mg/kg/day) until afebrile for 48 hours, then switch to low-dose aspirin (3-5 mg/kg/day) until inflammatory markers and echocardiogram are normal
new_options.rationale: He meets clinical criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 principal features) and remains within the day-10 window where IVIG most reduces coronary aneurysm risk. Australian practice (RCH CPG) uses IVIG 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin acutely, stepping down to low-dose antiplatelet aspirin once afebrile and continuing until coronary status is confirmed.

new_options.text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day as primary adjunct; withhold aspirin because of risk of Reye syndrome in a child under 5
new_options.rationale: Aspirin is standard in Kawasaki disease and the Reye-syndrome concern does not override its use in this indication. Adjunctive steroids are reserved for high-risk patients (Kobayashi or Egami score, age under 12 months, persistent fever) or IVIG-resistant disease, not first-line in this child, per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 10 days for presumed group A streptococcal scarlet fever
new_options.rationale: Scarlet fever does not cause non-purulent conjunctivitis or extremity desquamation; missing Kawasaki disease delays IVIG and increases aneurysm risk per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Defer IVIG until echocardiography is completed and confirms coronary changes, then treat
new_options.rationale: IVIG should be given on clinical grounds within 10 days of fever onset; echocardiography is for baseline assessment of coronaries, not a gate to treatment, per RCH CPG and AHA 2017.

new_options.text: Reassurance and antipyretics; arrange paediatric outpatient review if fever persists beyond day 10
new_options.rationale: Delaying treatment beyond day 10 substantially increases coronary aneurysm risk; treatment should be commenced now, per RCH CPG.

verdicts.discard_reason: Pure recall of the ANZCOR single-rescuer paediatric BLS ratio (30:2). The topic does not support genuine alternative-selection, anti-pattern, AU-specific cut-off discrimination, or multi-step decision-making; it is a memorised number. Reasoning lever options were considered (e.g. dispatcher-assisted CPR vs unwitnessed-arrest decisions) but each collapses to the same memorised algorithm step.

verdicts.new_stem: A previously well 5-year-old boy presents to his GP with 4 days of fever, productive cough and reduced activity. He is fully immunised on the Australian NIP including 13vPCV, and has had no foreign travel or sick contacts. He is alert, well perfused, taking oral fluids and about 75 percent of usual food, with no recessions or grunting. SpO2 is 96 percent on room air. Auscultation reveals bronchial breathing and crackles in the right lower zone. A chest X-ray performed because of the persistent fever shows right lower lobe consolidation without effusion. His weight is 18 kg. He has no known drug allergies and has not received antibiotics in the previous 3 months.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which oral antibiotic regimen is most appropriate as outpatient first-line therapy?

new_options.text: Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Dose is too low; Therapeutic Guidelines and the AMH Children's Dosing Companion specify 25-30 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for paediatric CAP to cover pneumococci with reduced penicillin susceptibility.

new_options.text: Amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for 5 days
new_options.rationale: First-line for uncomplicated CAP in a well child tolerating oral therapy with SpO2 over 92 percent: oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for 5 days, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and RCH CPG: Pneumonia. Five days is the current AU-recommended duration for uncomplicated CAP.

new_options.text: Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days as first-line monotherapy
new_options.rationale: Macrolides are added only when atypical infection is suspected (often school-age with persistent fever or after first-line failure); they are not monotherapy first-line for typical lobar CAP, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

new_options.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5/3.2 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for aspiration pneumonia or after failure of amoxicillin; the added clavulanate is unnecessary and increases adverse effects, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

new_options.text: Cefalexin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Cefalexin has poorer activity than amoxicillin against penicillin-non-susceptible Streptococcus pneumoniae and is not first-line for paediatric CAP, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) on hydroxyurea and penicillin V prophylaxis presents with 3 days of fatigue, pallor and exertional dyspnoea. Two weeks ago her 5-year-old brother developed bright red cheeks with a lacy reticular rash on his arms and trunk after a coryzal prodrome. She has had no fever, no chest pain, no abdominal pain and no bone pain. She is fully immunised. Examination shows pallor and tachycardia without jaundice or splenomegaly, and a clear chest. Bloods show Hb 52 g/L (her usual baseline 78 g/L), MCV 86 fL, reticulocyte count 4 x 10^9/L (markedly reduced), WCC 6.2 x 10^9/L, platelets 240 x 10^9/L, and bilirubin and LDH at her usual baseline.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits the haematological pattern and exposure history?

new_options.text: Acute haemolytic exacerbation of sickle cell disease
new_options.rationale: An acute haemolytic event would raise the reticulocyte count, bilirubin and LDH; here the reticulocyte count is markedly reduced and LDH/bilirubin are at baseline, making this incompatible.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

new_options.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis
new_options.rationale: Sequestration produces rapid splenic enlargement and a fall in Hb in young children; this child has no splenomegaly and HbSS spleens typically autoinfarct by school age, making sequestration unlikely.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

new_options.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from suboptimal dietary intake
new_options.rationale: Iron deficiency is microcytic and develops over weeks; her MCV is 86 fL and the Hb has dropped acutely, so this does not fit.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Iron deficiency anaemia

new_options.text: Acute chest syndrome
new_options.rationale: ACS requires a new pulmonary infiltrate with one of fever, chest pain or hypoxia; she has a clear chest, no chest pain, no fever and SpO2 98 percent on room air.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Sickle cell disease

new_options.text: Transient red cell aplasia from parvovirus B19 infection
new_options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid progenitors and stops erythropoiesis for about 7-10 days. In a host with a shortened red cell lifespan (HbSS), this manifests as severe normocytic anaemia with a markedly reduced reticulocyte count and unchanged haemolytic markers, exactly as seen here. The exposure (sibling with slapped-cheek 2 weeks earlier) fits, per BMJ Best Practice and RCH CPG.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old previously well, fully immunised girl is brought to her GP with 12 hours of right ear pain and a mild fever. She has been clingy but is eating and drinking normally with no vomiting and no neck stiffness. This is her first episode and she has no high-risk background (no cleft palate, no immunodeficiency, not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander). On examination her left tympanic membrane is normal and her right tympanic membrane is erythematous and bulging with the membrane intact and no otorrhoea. She is afebrile in clinic at 37.8 deg C. Her mother reports she settles with paracetamol.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

new_options.text: Regular analgesia (paracetamol with or without ibuprofen) and safety-net advice to return or recommence antibiotics if not improving at 48 to 72 hours
new_options.rationale: Australian guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic, RCH CPG: AOM) reserves antibiotics for children under 6 months always, under 2 years with bilateral disease, otorrhoea, systemically unwell, or high-risk groups (Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander background, immunocompromised, cochlear implants). A low-risk, well, over-2 child with unilateral AOM and an intact TM is managed with analgesia and watchful waiting.

new_options.text: Immediate oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days
new_options.rationale: The AU dose when antibiotics are indicated for AOM is amoxicillin 30 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for 5 days, not 15 mg/kg; and in this low-risk child antibiotics are not first-line, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

new_options.text: Immediate oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5/3.2 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is for amoxicillin failure, recent amoxicillin use, or recurrent AOM, not a first uncomplicated episode, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

new_options.text: Immediate oral cefaclor 10 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Cefaclor is poorly active against penicillin-non-susceptible pneumococcus and risks serum-sickness-like reactions; it is not first-line, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

new_options.text: Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops twice daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Topical quinolone drops are for AOM with perforation, otorrhoea through a grommet, or chronic suppurative OM; the TM here is intact, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to his GP for a pre-kinder health check. He is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile, fully immunised, with no exercise intolerance, syncope, cyanosis or breathlessness on play. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death or congenital heart disease, no recent sore throats, no joint pains, and no rashes. On examination he is pink and well perfused with normal femoral pulses, no thrill, a quiet S1 and S2 with no fixed splitting, and a soft musical murmur best heard at the lower left sternal edge while supine. The murmur has a distinctly vibratory or twangy quality, occupies early to mid-systole, is grade 2/6 in intensity, softens considerably on sitting up, and almost disappears with the Valsalva manoeuvre. There is no diastolic component and no radiation to the neck or axilla.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Reassure the family that this is a Still's vibratory innocent murmur and document review at routine health checks; no investigations or paediatric cardiology referral are required
new_options.rationale: Vibratory grade 1-2 early systolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge in a well 2-7-year-old that softens with Valsalva and on sitting is the classic Still's murmur. Per RCH CPG: Heart murmurs, an asymptomatic child with all innocent-murmur features (no diastolic component, no thrill, soft, varies with posture, normal pulses) does not require ECG, echo or referral.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Heart murmurs in children

new_options.text: Refer for paediatric cardiology assessment and echocardiogram because innocent murmurs cannot be distinguished from pathological murmurs on examination alone
new_options.rationale: Australian primary-care guidance accepts the diagnosis of a Still's murmur clinically when all innocent features are present; routine echo is not required, per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Arrange ECG and ASOT today and treat as possible acute rheumatic carditis pending results
new_options.rationale: There are no Jones features and no sore throat history; investigating for ARF in an asymptomatic child with a classic innocent murmur is inappropriate, per RHDAustralia guideline.

new_options.text: Arrange exercise stress testing to exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy before clearing for kindergarten sport
new_options.rationale: HCM typically produces a harsh systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva (the opposite of this murmur) and there is no family history of sudden cardiac death; exercise testing is not indicated, per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Document the murmur as a small ventricular septal defect and arrange echocardiogram within one month
new_options.rationale: A small VSD murmur is harsh and pansystolic, often with a thrill, and does not soften with Valsalva; the murmur described is musical, dynamic with posture, and innocent, per RCH CPG.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred to a community paediatrician for short stature and a heart murmur. He was born at term with a birthweight of 3.2 kg. Height has tracked along the 3rd centile and weight on the 10th centile, despite a mid-parental height on the 50th centile. He sat unsupported at 9 months, walked at 18 months and now uses only 2 to 3 word phrases. He has hypertelorism with downslanting palpebral fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, a webbed neck with low posterior hairline, and a pectus carinatum superiorly with pectus excavatum inferiorly. There is a 3/6 ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge that is loudest on inspiration, with a soft single S2 and no thrill. Four-limb blood pressures are symmetric and femoral pulses are equal. Echocardiography shows a thickened, dysplastic pulmonary valve with a peak gradient of 45 mmHg, mild post-stenotic dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, and no left ventricular hypertrophy.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of underlying diagnosis and confirmatory genetic test is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Williams syndrome; request fluorescence in situ hybridisation for the 7q11.23 elastin region
new_options.rationale: Williams syndrome features supravalvular aortic stenosis (right upper sternal edge, radiates to neck) and elfin facies; this child has dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis with Noonan facies.

new_options.text: Turner syndrome; request peripheral blood karyotype with 30-cell count for mosaicism
new_options.rationale: Turner syndrome affects phenotypic females and features bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation; this patient is male with pulmonary stenosis.

new_options.text: Noonan syndrome; request a multi-gene RASopathy panel (PTPN11, SOS1, RAF1 and related genes)
new_options.rationale: Dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis (50 percent of cases) with short stature, hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, webbed neck, pectus carinatum/excavatum and mild developmental delay is the Noonan phenotype. PTPN11 mutations are responsible for about half of cases, but several RASopathy genes can produce the phenotype, so a multi-gene panel (not single-gene PTPN11) is the appropriate first-line genetic test per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Marfan syndrome; request FBN1 sequencing
new_options.rationale: Marfan syndrome features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation; short stature, webbed neck and pulmonary stenosis do not fit.

new_options.text: Trisomy 21; request peripheral blood karyotype
new_options.rationale: Down syndrome features upslanting palpebral fissures, single palmar crease and AVSD; this child's downslanting fissures and pulmonary valve dysplasia do not fit.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred for assessment of moderate intellectual disability (recent WISC FSIQ 58), comorbid attentional difficulties, hand-flapping when excited, poor eye contact, echolalia and tactile defensiveness. He attends mainstream school with full-time aide support. Examination shows a long narrow face with prominent forehead, large protruding ears, a high-arched palate, hyperextensible joints, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, pes planus, and symmetrically enlarged testes at a Prader orchidometer volume of 6 mL bilaterally for prepubertal age. There is no microcephaly and no neurocutaneous stigmata. Family history shows a maternal uncle with late-onset intention tremor and gait disorder diagnosed in his 60s, and a maternal grandmother with primary ovarian insufficiency at age 36. Microarray comparative genomic hybridisation at age 4 was normal and a metabolic screen including very long chain fatty acids was normal.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which diagnosis-and-investigation pair best fits and would have been missed by the prior microarray?

new_options.text: Klinefelter syndrome; peripheral blood karyotype
new_options.rationale: Klinefelter features tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism), and milder cognitive impact; the family history of FXTAS and FXPOI is not explained.

new_options.text: Williams syndrome; FISH for 7q11.23 elastin
new_options.rationale: Williams syndrome features elfin facies, supravalvular AS, hypercalcaemia and overfriendly personality; macroorchidism and the X-linked family pattern are not explained, and a 7q deletion would have shown on the prior microarray.

new_options.text: Prader-Willi syndrome; DNA methylation analysis at 15q11-q13
new_options.rationale: Prader-Willi features neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia, obesity and hypogonadism with small testes; this profile of macroorchidism and intellectual disability does not fit.

new_options.text: Tuberous sclerosis complex; TSC1 and TSC2 sequencing
new_options.rationale: TSC features hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas and epilepsy; none are present.

new_options.text: Fragile X syndrome; targeted FMR1 PCR with methylation analysis
new_options.rationale: Moderate ID with autistic features, long face, prominent ears, joint hypermobility, macroorchidism (already enlarged prepubertally), a maternal uncle with FXTAS and a maternal grandmother with FXPOI fits Fragile X (FMR1 CGG expansion over 200 with methylation). Microarrays do not detect CGG trinucleotide expansions, which is why the prior microarray was normal; FMR1 targeted testing with methylation analysis is the correct next investigation, per RCH CPG: Fragile X.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

verdicts.new_stem: A 13-year-old male competitive basketballer presents to his GP with a 6-week history of bilateral anterior knee pain that is worse during and after training, jumping and stair climbing, and that settles with rest. He denies any acute injury and has had no swelling, locking or giving way. He has had a recent growth spurt of 8 cm over the past 12 months. On examination both knees show localised tenderness and a palpable bony prominence at the tibial tuberosity, with reproduction of pain on resisted knee extension and on direct pressure over the tuberosity. There is no joint effusion, no warmth, no ligamentous laxity and no patellar grind. He has full painless range, no nocturnal pain, no constitutional symptoms and no inguinal lymphadenopathy. He is afebrile with normal observations.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Reassure with the clinical diagnosis of Osgood-Schlatter, advise relative activity modification, ice and simple analgesia, and continue sport within pain tolerance
new_options.rationale: Bilateral tibial tuberosity tenderness in an adolescent during a growth spurt with reproducible pain on resisted knee extension and no red flags is diagnostic of Osgood-Schlatter (traction apophysitis); imaging is not required and management is conservative, per RCH CPG: Knee pain.

new_options.text: Order bilateral knee MRI to exclude osteochondritis dissecans and refer to paediatric orthopaedics
new_options.rationale: MRI and orthopaedic referral are reserved for atypical presentations (unilateral, nocturnal pain, locking, persistent symptoms beyond skeletal maturity); typical bilateral apophysitis does not need imaging.

new_options.text: Apply long-leg plaster casts bilaterally for 6 weeks then reassess for ongoing apophyseal fragmentation
new_options.rationale: Casting is not recommended for Osgood-Schlatter and risks deconditioning; relative rest with continued activity within pain tolerance preserves strength and growth.

new_options.text: Inject intralesional corticosteroid into the tibial tuberosity bilaterally to reduce inflammation
new_options.rationale: Intralesional corticosteroid near an open apophysis risks soft tissue atrophy, depigmentation and growth disturbance.

new_options.text: Arrange plain knee radiographs in both AP and skyline views to confirm the diagnosis and exclude tibial tuberosity avulsion fracture
new_options.rationale: Plain films are not routine for typical Osgood-Schlatter; tuberosity fragmentation on X-ray is a normal variant in adolescents and does not change management. Imaging is reserved for atypical features (acute injury, locking, persistent unilateral pain beyond skeletal maturity).

verdicts.new_stem: A term female infant is being examined on day 2 of life on the postnatal ward. She was born by elective caesarean at 39+1 weeks for breech presentation that did not respond to external cephalic version. Birthweight was 3520 g. There is a family history of developmental dysplasia of the hip in her older sister who required a Pavlik harness. She is breastfeeding well and has passed urine and meconium. On hip examination today, the Ortolani manoeuvre on the left hip elicits a palpable clunk as the femoral head reduces into the acetabulum with abduction. The right hip is stable. There is no leg-length discrepancy or asymmetric thigh skin creases. Neurological examination including primitive reflexes is normal.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Reassure that hip clunks at this age are usually benign and arrange a routine 6-week paediatric review without specialist input or imaging today
new_options.rationale: A positive Ortolani is a clinically dislocated and reducible hip, not a benign click; same-admission paediatric orthopaedic referral is mandatory per RCH CPG: DDH.

new_options.text: Order an immediate plain anteroposterior pelvic radiograph to assess acetabular index and the Shenton line before referral
new_options.rationale: Plain radiographs are unreliable before about 4-6 months because the femoral heads have not ossified; ultrasound (static and dynamic, Graf classification) is the neonatal imaging modality, per RCH CPG: DDH.

new_options.text: Refer to paediatric orthopaedic surgery now for assessment and Pavlik harness application, and arrange a static and dynamic hip ultrasound
new_options.rationale: A frankly positive Ortolani with risk factors (female, breech, family history) mandates urgent paediatric orthopaedic referral, supervised Pavlik harness fitting, and ultrasound (Graf classification, static and dynamic) for morphology, per RCH CPG: DDH and the Australian national hip surveillance guidance.

new_options.text: Apply double nappies between the legs to maintain abduction and review the infant in 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: Double-nappying is not evidence-based and delays definitive Pavlik harness treatment in a frankly dislocated hip.

new_options.text: Arrange an outpatient hip ultrasound at 6 weeks as standard risk-factor screening
new_options.rationale: The 6-week screening pathway applies to at-risk infants with a normal clinical examination; a positive Ortolani is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate referral and harness, not delayed screening.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the GP after the third episode of right-sided epistaxis this month. Each episode has been brief (5 to 10 minutes), stopped with pressure, and has occurred when he was picking his nose. He is otherwise well, with no bruising, no bleeding from other sites and no family history of bleeding disorders. He is on no medication. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a small visible vessel and a crust over the right Little area, with no active bleeding; the left nasal cavity is clear. There is no septal haematoma, no mass and no facial deformity. He has full eye movements, no proptosis, no neck masses and no lymphadenopathy. Full blood count and coagulation screen are normal.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Refer urgently to ENT for examination under general anaesthetic to exclude juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
new_options.rationale: JNA affects adolescent males with unilateral nasal obstruction and severe recurrent epistaxis often with a nasopharyngeal mass; digit-trauma anterior bleeding in a 6-year-old is not the JNA pattern.

new_options.text: Apply topical antiseptic ointment (Naseptin or paraffin) twice daily for 2 weeks plus reinforce first aid technique (pinch the soft part of the nose, lean forward, 10 to 15 minutes)
new_options.rationale: Recurrent anterior epistaxis at Little area in a child is most often due to digit trauma and mucosal dryness. First-line management is topical antiseptic ointment plus reinforced first aid, which reduces recurrence in randomised trials. Silver nitrate cautery is reserved for failure of conservative care, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.

new_options.text: Perform bedside silver nitrate cautery bilaterally to Little area today with topical antibiotic ointment for 1 week
new_options.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks septal perforation; cautery (when needed) is unilateral and is generally a second-line ENT intervention after failed conservative therapy.

new_options.text: Insert an anterior Merocel nasal pack into the right nostril and remove at 48 hours in the GP rooms
new_options.rationale: Packing is uncomfortable in children and is reserved for ongoing bleeding not controlled by first aid; it is not appropriate as outpatient management of recurrent self-limited bleeds.

new_options.text: Commence oral tranexamic acid 25 mg/kg three times daily for 6 weeks as antifibrinolytic prophylaxis
new_options.rationale: Oral tranexamic acid has no role as first-line outpatient management of mucosal-trauma epistaxis with normal coagulation in a child.

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-year-old girl presents to her GP with a 3-day history of a tender red swelling in front of the right ear. Her mother reports a small pit in front of the right ear since birth that has occasionally discharged a small amount of fluid but has never been red or swollen before. On examination there is a 1.5 cm tender, erythematous, fluctuant swelling immediately anterior to the right ear at the level of the helical root, centred over a visible pit. The pinna, ear canal and tympanic membrane are normal. There is no facial nerve weakness, no cervical lymphadenopathy and no parotid swelling. She is afebrile and otherwise well. She has no penicillin allergy.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan now, and what is the appropriate timing of definitive surgery?

new_options.text: Oral di/flucloxacillin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for 7 days now, with incision and drainage if not settling at 48 to 72 hours; refer to ENT for elective complete sinus excision once the acute infection has fully resolved (typically 6 weeks)
new_options.rationale: An acutely infected pre-auricular sinus is treated with anti-staphylococcal oral antibiotic and I&D if abscess fails to settle. Definitive excision is delayed until after the acute inflammation resolves because operating on inflamed tissue increases recurrence; the planes are clearer at 4-6 weeks, per RCH CPG: Pre-auricular sinus and Therapeutic Guidelines.

new_options.text: Urgent total parotidectomy on suspicion of an infected first branchial cleft anomaly with facial nerve involvement
new_options.rationale: First branchial cleft anomalies are rare and present with peri-auricular sinus tracts that may be intimately related to the facial nerve; a typical pre-auricular pit at the helical root does not warrant parotidectomy.

new_options.text: Reassurance and review only if the lesion enlarges or discharges further
new_options.rationale: An infected pre-auricular sinus requires antibiotic treatment and ENT referral for definitive excision; observation alone risks abscess progression and chronic discharge.

new_options.text: Express the sinus contents by firm squeezing today plus topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for 7 days
new_options.rationale: Squeezing spreads infection and damages adjacent tissues; systemic antibiotics with surgical drainage if needed is appropriate.

new_options.text: Same-day complete excision of the sinus tract under general anaesthesia with antibiotic cover
new_options.rationale: Operating on an acutely inflamed sinus increases recurrence because tissue planes are obscured; excision is deferred until the acute infection has settled, per RCH CPG.

verdicts.new_stem: A 9-month-old girl is brought to the GP after her grandmother noticed that the left pupil appeared white in a recent flash photograph. The mother reports the eye otherwise looks normal, the baby fixes and follows in good light and reaches for toys with both hands. She was born at term by normal vaginal birth with an unremarkable antenatal course. The parents do not recall whether the red reflex was specifically examined on the newborn check. There is no family history of retinoblastoma or other eye disease. On examination with an ophthalmoscope held at arm's length the right pupil shows a normal red reflex but the left pupil shows a clearly visible white reflex (leukocoria). There is no strabismus, no nystagmus and no proptosis. The cornea is clear and the anterior chamber appears normal. Visual behaviour is reduced when the right eye is occluded.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Reassure that white reflex on flash photographs is usually benign and review at the routine 12-month immunisation visit
new_options.rationale: Leukocoria is never normal and mandates same-day to next-day paediatric ophthalmology assessment to exclude retinoblastoma and other sight-threatening or life-threatening pathology, per RANZCO position statement on red reflex testing.

new_options.text: Refer to a community optometrist for paediatric vision testing in 4 to 6 weeks
new_options.rationale: Optometry deferral risks unacceptable delay; retinoblastoma is curable when caught early but devastating when missed.

new_options.text: Refer urgently (within 24 to 48 hours) to a paediatric ophthalmologist for examination under anaesthetic to assess for retinoblastoma and other causes of leukocoria
new_options.rationale: Persistent unilateral leukocoria in an infant requires urgent paediatric ophthalmology referral. EUA is the diagnostic step; differential includes retinoblastoma, congenital cataract, persistent foetal vasculature, Coats disease and toxocariasis. Imaging is staging not screening, per RANZCO and RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Order outpatient MRI brain and orbits under GA as the initial investigation to characterise any intraocular mass before specialist ophthalmology referral
new_options.rationale: MRI is for staging once retinoblastoma is diagnosed (optic nerve extension, trilateral disease); the first step is paediatric ophthalmology EUA, not pre-emptive imaging. Imaging-before-EUA is the anti-pattern tested here.

new_options.text: Patch the right (unaffected) eye for 2 hours daily for amblyopia management of the left eye and review in 3 months
new_options.rationale: Patching is for confirmed amblyopia after the underlying lesion has been identified and treated; occlusion now could delay diagnosis of retinoblastoma.

verdicts.new_stem: An 18-month-old girl is brought to her GP because her grandmother thinks she has a squint. The mother is unsure. She was born at term, met milestones and has otherwise been well. There is no family history of strabismus, amblyopia or significant refractive error. On examination she has a broad nasal bridge and prominent epicanthic folds. Hirschberg corneal light reflex test is centred symmetrically in both pupils. The cover-uncover test reveals no movement of either eye when the other is occluded. Eye movements are full in all directions of gaze. Red reflex is normal bilaterally and visual behaviour is age-appropriate with good fixation and following.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric ophthalmology for examination under anaesthesia and consideration of strabismus surgery
new_options.rationale: There is no true strabismus on Hirschberg or cover-uncover testing; this is pseudoesotropia and does not require surgery.

new_options.text: Reassure that this is pseudoesotropia from epicanthic folds, document normal Hirschberg and cover-uncover testing, and book the routine 4-year preschool vision screen; advise return if any new objective squint is noted
new_options.rationale: Symmetric corneal light reflex and a negative cover-uncover test with epicanthic folds is pseudoesotropia; the apparent squint resolves as facial features mature. Reassurance with routine preschool screen is appropriate, per RANZCO and RCH CPG: Vision screening.

new_options.text: Patch the left eye for 2 hours daily for 3 months to prevent amblyopia
new_options.rationale: Patching is for confirmed amblyopia after specialist diagnosis; occluding a child without amblyopia is harmful.

new_options.text: Commence topical atropine 1% to the right eye to penalise the dominant eye for presumed left amblyopia
new_options.rationale: Atropine penalisation is for confirmed amblyopia; it has no role in a child with normal vision and no strabismus.

new_options.text: Order outpatient MRI brain and orbits under sedation to exclude a sixth nerve palsy
new_options.rationale: A sixth nerve palsy would cause limitation of abduction and incomitant esotropia; full eye movements and a negative cover-uncover test exclude this.

verdicts.new_stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to his GP with recurrent severe right-sided epistaxis over 4 months. Each episode lasts over 20 minutes and on two occasions has required ED attendance for anterior packing. He also reports 3 months of progressive right-sided nasal obstruction and intermittent right cheek fullness. There is no history of trauma, no significant nose picking and no family history of bleeding disorders. He is on no medication. He is otherwise well and growing along the 50th centile. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a pink-grey mass filling the posterior right nasal cavity that bled briskly on attempted swab. Full blood count and coagulation screen are normal.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Reassure as benign anterior Little area epistaxis and prescribe topical Naseptin twice daily for 2 weeks with first-aid advice
new_options.rationale: Severe recurrent unilateral epistaxis with nasal obstruction and a visible posterior mass must not be treated as benign anterior bleeding; topical antiseptic alone misses JNA.

new_options.text: Attempt bedside biopsy of the visible posterior mass with cup forceps under topical lignocaine to confirm histology before imaging or specialist referral
new_options.rationale: JNA is exceptionally vascular and clinic biopsy can cause life-threatening haemorrhage; biopsy-before-imaging is the anti-pattern tested here. Imaging precedes any biopsy and biopsy occurs in a controlled surgical environment.

new_options.text: Refer urgently to ENT and arrange contrast-enhanced MRI or CT of the nasopharynx without biopsy in clinic
new_options.rationale: Recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis with progressive nasal obstruction and a posterior nasal mass in an adolescent male is the classic JNA triad. Urgent ENT referral with contrast cross-sectional imaging is required; biopsy is performed in theatre after imaging and angiographic assessment if needed, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis and BMJ Best Practice.

new_options.text: Apply silver nitrate cautery to both sides of the nasal septum at Little area today plus topical antibiotic ointment and GP review in 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks perforation and does not address a posterior nasopharyngeal mass.

new_options.text: Commence oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily for 6 weeks as definitive antifibrinolytic therapy
new_options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct only and does not substitute for ENT assessment and imaging of a suspected vascular nasopharyngeal tumour.

verdicts.new_stem: A previously well 5-year-old girl is brought to a Melbourne general practice with a 4-day history of crusted sores around the nose and right cheek that began after a minor scratch in the playground. She is systemically well and attending school. She has no drug allergies, no eczema and no household members with skin disease. She is afebrile with normal vital signs. On examination there are three discrete lesions less than 3 cm in total area with classic honey-coloured crust, no surrounding cellulitis, no bullae, no lymphangitis and palpable but non-tender submandibular nodes under 1 cm. There is no mucosal involvement and no other skin lesions. She lives in a non-remote setting; the family does not identify as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. There has been no recent overseas travel and there is no local MRSA outbreak.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

new_options.text: Topical mupirocin 2% ointment to lesions three times daily for 5 days, with lesions kept covered or to remain home until lesions are dry; no oral antibiotics
new_options.rationale: Localised non-bullous impetigo in a low-prevalence non-remote setting (less than 3 small lesion areas) is treated topically with mupirocin or hydrogen peroxide cream per eTG. School exclusion until lesions are covered or have crusted; oral antibiotics are reserved for extensive disease, systemic illness, or high-prevalence/remote/Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander community settings where oral therapy is first-line, per eTG and RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg up to 500 mg twice daily for 7 days plus daily chlorhexidine washes for the household
new_options.rationale: First-line oral when oral therapy is indicated is flucloxacillin (or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in high-MRSA settings); cefalexin is broader than required, and oral therapy is not indicated here per eTG.

new_options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4/20 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days as empirical community-acquired MRSA cover
new_options.rationale: Empirical MRSA cover is reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected MRSA (e.g. high-prevalence remote or Top End/central Australia settings); not first-line in a Melbourne low-prevalence setting.

new_options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg up to 2 g six-hourly with admission for paediatric observation
new_options.rationale: IV therapy and admission are for severe cellulitis, abscess, systemic toxicity or oral therapy failure, none of which apply.

new_options.text: Reassurance and observation only with return at 7 days if not resolved
new_options.rationale: Impetigo is contagious and topical treatment shortens duration and limits spread to household and school contacts.

verdicts.new_stem: A 10-month-old fully immunised infant is reviewed at general practice. He has had 3 days of fever to 39.6 deg C without other localising symptoms. He fed reasonably and remained alert between fever spikes. Today the fever has settled and a pink, blanching macular and maculopapular rash has appeared on the trunk, spreading sparsely to the neck and proximal limbs while sparing the face, palms and soles. He has no conjunctivitis, no Koplik spots, no cough or coryza, no lymphadenopathy and no oral lesions. His mother had no febrile illness in pregnancy. Vitals are normal for age. There is no travel history and the only known sick contact is an older sibling at daycare. He has no eczema and no chronic illness. The MMR vaccine is not yet due at his age.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which clinical pattern is most specific and supports the most likely diagnosis?

new_options.text: Rash starting on the face and spreading caudally, with conjunctivitis and Koplik spots, supports measles
new_options.rationale: Measles requires fever PLUS cough, coryza or conjunctivitis with Koplik spots and a face-down spreading rash. None of these features are present, so measles is not supported by this pattern.

new_options.text: Defervescence preceding the appearance of a truncal blanching maculopapular rash that spares the face in a 6-24 month-old supports roseola infantum (HHV-6)
new_options.rationale: The classic temporal sequence of HHV-6 roseola is 3-5 days of high fever in a 6-24 month-old, defervescence, and then a truncal blanching maculopapular rash that spares the face and palms/soles - exactly the pattern here. The temporal sequence is the discriminating feature, per RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

new_options.text: Tender post-auricular or sub-occipital lymphadenopathy with a face-down spreading rash supports rubella
new_options.rationale: Rubella features post-auricular lymphadenopathy and a face-down rash, neither present here.

new_options.text: A bright facial slapped-cheek appearance followed by a lacy reticular rash on the limbs supports parvovirus B19
new_options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 typically affects school-age children with a slapped-cheek facial rash and a lacy limb rash, not a truncal rash sparing the face in an infant.

new_options.text: A fine sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and perioral pallor preceded by sore throat supports scarlet fever
new_options.rationale: Scarlet fever requires GAS pharyngitis features which are absent.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to a GP clinic with one day of low-grade fever (38.1 deg C), reduced oral intake and new tender oral and skin lesions. Her older brother attended childcare with a known outbreak of hand foot and mouth disease last week. She is fully immunised and previously well. On examination she has several shallow oral ulcers on the soft palate and buccal mucosa and small grey-roofed vesicles surrounded by an erythematous halo on the palms, soles and around the buttocks. There is no rash on the trunk or scalp, no lymphadenopathy, no conjunctivitis and no genital or perianal blistering. Vitals are otherwise normal. Her mother is 30 weeks pregnant and asks specifically when the child can return to childcare.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate diagnosis-and-exclusion advice per current Australian guidance?

new_options.text: Hand foot and mouth disease; no formal exclusion is required under NHMRC Staying Healthy provided the child feels well enough to participate; advise good hand hygiene and cover any weeping lesions
new_options.rationale: Under NHMRC Staying Healthy (6th edition, current Australian guidance), formal exclusion is not required for HFMD provided the child is well enough to participate; advice focuses on hand hygiene and covering weeping lesions. Older 'until all blisters have dried' advice has been updated; this is the genuine AU-cut-off discrimination.

new_options.text: Hand foot and mouth disease; exclude until all blisters have completely dried, regardless of how well the child is
new_options.rationale: This is the older blanket exclusion rule, superseded in the current NHMRC Staying Healthy guidance.

new_options.text: Primary herpes simplex gingivostomatitis; exclude until afebrile and lesions crusted
new_options.rationale: Primary HSV gingivostomatitis produces clustered anterior oral vesicles, gingival inflammation and bleeding without acral palm and sole lesions.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Cold sores and gingivostomatitis

new_options.text: Varicella; exclude for at least 5 days from rash onset and until all lesions have crusted
new_options.rationale: Varicella produces crops of vesicles on trunk, face and scalp evolving through stages, not an acral vesicular eruption sparing the trunk.

new_options.text: Erythema multiforme minor; no exclusion required as the condition is non-infectious
new_options.rationale: EM has target lesions on limbs without the palm-sole-buccal pattern, and the childcare exposure with classic distribution makes HFMD overwhelmingly likely.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-year-old girl is seen with a 24-hour history of bilateral facial rash following a mild flu-like illness last week. She has had no fever, is eating normally and is attending school. On examination she has a confluent red rash on both cheeks with circumoral sparing and a faint lacy, reticular rash on the extensor surfaces of both forearms. She is afebrile with normal vital signs. Her mother is 22 weeks pregnant and her younger brother has known sickle cell disease (HbSS) with a baseline haemoglobin of 78 g/L. The school has reported recent cases of slapped cheek disease. There is no recent overseas travel.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which household contact warrants the most urgent specific action, and what is the appropriate first investigation for that contact?

new_options.text: All family members: reassure that the index case is no longer infectious now that the rash has appeared; no further action required
new_options.rationale: While the index case is no longer infectious after rash onset, household contacts were exposed during the preceding viraemic week; the brother with sickle cell disease and the pregnant mother both need targeted action.

new_options.text: Brother with sickle cell disease: clinical review and urgent FBC plus reticulocyte count; arrange admission for transfusion if Hb is markedly below baseline with reticulocytopenia (transient aplastic crisis)
new_options.rationale: Parvovirus B19 arrests erythroid precursors and precipitates transient red cell aplasia in patients with shortened red cell lifespan. The brother with HbSS is at highest immediate risk and requires urgent FBC plus reticulocyte count; transfusion is required if Hb has dropped substantially with reticulocytopenia. The pregnant mother needs serology and obstetric review but on a less immediate timeline (days, not hours), per RCH CPG and RANZCOG.

new_options.text: Mother: immediate empirical IVIG to prevent foetal hydrops irrespective of her immunity status
new_options.rationale: First-line for maternal exposure is parvovirus IgG/IgM serology; if seroconverting, serial foetal ultrasound with MCA-PSV for foetal anaemia is used, not empirical IVIG.

new_options.text: Mother: termination counselling because of high foetal loss risk in the second trimester
new_options.rationale: Foetal loss risk is highest in the first 20 weeks but remains under 10 percent overall; management is serology and surveillance, not termination counselling.

new_options.text: Index patient: exclude from school for a further 5 days because she is now most infectious
new_options.rationale: Viraemia has resolved by the time the slapped-cheek rash appears; the child is no longer infectious and exclusion is not required.

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-year-old boy is reviewed in a community allergy clinic after two reactions to egg: hives and lip swelling, and a separate episode of vomiting plus audible wheeze within 20 minutes of ingestion. One reaction received adrenaline in the ED. There has been no hypotension or collapse. Skin prick test today shows an 8 mm wheal to egg white and specific IgE to egg white is 22 kU/L. He has mild persistent asthma well controlled on a low-dose ICS. He weighs 17 kg. He has no other allergies. The family has not previously been prescribed an adrenaline autoinjector. Both parents have completed asthma first aid training and the childcare requires a written ASCIA plan.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of ASCIA action plan and autoinjector strength is correct?

new_options.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions (non-anaphylaxis plan), no autoinjector, oral cetirizine PRN
new_options.rationale: Documented wheeze with food trigger is anaphylaxis; non-anaphylaxis plans are for confirmed allergy without anaphylaxis and without prescribed autoinjector. Asthma comorbidity is an additional reason to prescribe adrenaline.

new_options.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis with a 150 microgram adrenaline autoinjector (EpiPen Jr) given his current weight of 17 kg
new_options.rationale: ASCIA prescribes 150 microgram (EpiPen Jr) for weight 7.5 to 20 kg and 300 microgram (EpiPen) for over 20 kg (with 500 microgram for over 50 kg). He weighs 17 kg, so 150 microgram is correct. Documented anaphylaxis with respiratory involvement plus asthma is an indication for an autoinjector and Anaphylaxis Action Plan.

new_options.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis with a 300 microgram autoinjector (EpiPen)
new_options.rationale: 300 microgram is prescribed from 20 kg upwards under ASCIA guidance; at 17 kg the 150 microgram device is recommended.

new_options.text: Refer for supervised oral food challenge to egg with no action plan until the challenge result is known
new_options.rationale: Recent objective anaphylaxis with a large SPT wheal and elevated specific IgE makes confirmatory challenge unsafe and unnecessary; the priority is the action plan and autoinjector.

new_options.text: Discharge with an ASCIA Asthma First Aid Plan only, treating the wheeze as asthma-driven
new_options.rationale: Wheeze within 20 minutes of a known food trigger is anaphylaxis until proven otherwise.

verdicts.new_stem: A 5-week-old first-born male is brought to the ED with a 10-day history of vomiting after every feed, now forceful and arching over the shoulder. The vomit is milky, never green or blood-stained. He is hungry immediately after vomiting and feeds vigorously. Birth was 39+4 NVD, weight 3.6 kg. Today his weight is 3.5 kg (50th to 3rd centile cross), capillary refill 3 seconds, anterior fontanelle slightly sunken. A 1 cm mobile firm mass is palpable in the right epigastrium after a test feed, with visible peristalsis crossing the upper abdomen left to right. Venous blood gas: pH 7.51, pCO2 50 mmHg, HCO3 38 mmol/L, Na 134, K 3.1, Cl 89, glucose 4.2 mmol/L. The paediatric surgical registrar wants to know the next step.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

new_options.text: Transfer to theatre for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after a single 20 mL/kg saline bolus without first correcting the alkalosis or chloride
new_options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is never a surgical emergency. Operating on an alkalotic, hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic infant risks post-anaesthetic apnoea from compensatory CSF alkalosis. Surgery-before-electrolyte-correction is the anti-pattern tested here.

new_options.text: Resuscitate with 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg, then maintenance and replacement with 0.45% saline plus 5% dextrose plus potassium until Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30, K over 3.5 mmol/L; confirmatory ultrasound; then pyloromyotomy
new_options.rationale: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before pyloromyotomy. AU-recommended targets are Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30, K over 3.5 mmol/L. Ultrasound (muscle thickness over 3 mm, channel length over 14 mm) confirms diagnosis, per RCH CPG and Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

new_options.text: Commence omeprazole 1 mg/kg orally and review in clinic in one week as outpatient reflux management
new_options.rationale: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with palpable olive and hypochloraemic alkalosis is pyloric stenosis, not GORD.

new_options.text: Request urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation before any fluid resuscitation or surgical referral
new_options.rationale: Vomiting is non-bilious and features are diagnostic of pyloric stenosis; upper GI contrast is for suspected malrotation with bilious vomiting.

new_options.text: Trial extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 weeks as cow's milk protein allergy is the most likely diagnosis
new_options.rationale: CMPA does not produce hypochloraemic alkalosis or a palpable olive; clinical picture is diagnostic of pyloric stenosis.

verdicts.new_stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with a 22-hour history of central abdominal pain that migrated to the right iliac fossa 8 hours ago. He has vomited twice, has not eaten since lunch yesterday, and has a temperature of 38.1 deg C. He is reluctant to walk and held his right hip flexed on arrival. There is no diarrhoea, no urinary symptoms, no foreign travel and no sick contacts. He is not on medication and is fully immunised. On examination HR 112, RR 22, BP 108/68, abdomen with localised tenderness at McBurney point, voluntary guarding without rebound, positive cough sign. There is no generalised peritonism. Bloods: WCC 14.8 x10^9/L (neutrophils 11.2), CRP 38 mg/L. Urinalysis is negative. Paediatric Appendicitis Score is 7/10. Radiology offers an ultrasound within the hour.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step, and what is the appropriate plan if ultrasound is non-visualising?

new_options.text: Discharge home with simple analgesia and safety-net advice given the absence of rebound peritonism
new_options.rationale: PAS 7 with WCC 14.8, CRP 38 and migratory pain is high-probability appendicitis; discharge is unsafe.

new_options.text: Order contrast CT abdomen and pelvis as the first-line imaging investigation
new_options.rationale: CT is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after ultrasound or when ultrasound is non-visualising in larger children; ultrasound is first-line in children to minimise ionising radiation.

new_options.text: Ultrasound RIF first; if positive (non-compressible blind-ending tubular structure over 6 mm with hyperaemia) proceed to laparoscopic appendicectomy with IV pre-operative antibiotics; if non-visualising, admit for serial clinical reassessment with repeat ultrasound or CT escalation as needed
new_options.rationale: Ultrasound is first-line paediatric imaging in Australia. A visualised appendix over 6 mm non-compressible with hyperaemia triggers laparoscopic appendicectomy with preoperative ceftriaxone plus metronidazole. Non-visualisation occurs in 20-40 percent and does not exclude appendicitis; serial examination (with or without CT escalation) is the correct next step rather than discharge, per RCH CPG and RANZCR.

new_options.text: Treat with IV cefotaxime and metronidazole as antibiotic-only therapy without surgical intervention
new_options.rationale: Antibiotic-only management is a research approach in selected uncomplicated cases and not standard Australian practice; confirmation of appendicitis must precede any non-operative trial.

new_options.text: Order MRI abdomen and pelvis as the first-line investigation to avoid ionising radiation
new_options.rationale: MRI is not routinely available out of hours and is not first-line; ultrasound gives the best speed-to-diagnosis ratio in children.

verdicts.new_stem: A 12-hour-old term female born by elective lower-segment caesarean for breech presentation at 39+2 weeks has had two episodes of bright-green vomiting after attempted breastfeeding. Antenatal scans at 12 and 20 weeks were reported as normal; nuchal translucency was low risk. Birth weight 3.1 kg, Apgars 9 and 9. She has not yet passed meconium. On examination HR 162, RR 56, T 36.8 deg C, SpO2 97% on room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen with a flat lower abdomen and visible epigastric fullness. There is no obvious mass, no inguinal hernia, and the anus is patent on inspection. A nasogastric tube is placed and aspirates 30 mL of bilious fluid. The on-call paediatric registrar is reviewing the supine abdominal radiograph and considering whether the appearance is consistent with duodenal atresia rather than malrotation with midgut volvulus.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which radiographic finding most reliably differentiates duodenal atresia from malrotation with midgut volvulus, and what is the implication for next investigation?

new_options.text: Multiple dilated small bowel loops with air-fluid levels and soap-bubble appearance, suggesting meconium ileus and prompting contrast enema
new_options.rationale: Soap-bubble (Neuhauser) appearance is meconium ileus, not duodenal atresia or volvulus.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

new_options.text: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas, suggesting necrotising enterocolitis
new_options.rationale: Pneumatosis and portal venous gas are NEC findings; clinically and radiographically distinct.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

new_options.text: Double bubble sign with absent distal bowel gas, supporting duodenal atresia; proceed to fluid resuscitation and surgical referral without urgent upper GI contrast
new_options.rationale: Duodenal atresia produces a classic double bubble (dilated stomach and proximal duodenum) with NO distal bowel gas. In contrast, malrotation with volvulus typically shows some distal gas with a paucity, and the diagnostic test is an URGENT upper GI contrast study showing corkscrew or duodenojejunal junction to the right of midline. Recognising the no-distal-gas double bubble as duodenal atresia therefore obviates upper GI contrast and allows direct fluid resuscitation and surgical referral, whereas any uncertainty mandates urgent contrast study, per RCH CPG.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

new_options.text: Free intraperitoneal gas under the diaphragm with a football sign, suggesting perforation
new_options.rationale: Free gas indicates perforation, not uncomplicated duodenal atresia or volvulus.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

new_options.text: Corkscrew appearance of the duodenum visible on the supine plain film
new_options.rationale: Corkscrew is a fluoroscopic finding on upper GI contrast in malrotation with volvulus, not a plain-film sign.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

verdicts.discard_reason: Pure recall of the NSW mandatory reporter framework. Stem already pre-commits the diagnosis ('disclosure of physical abuse with linear marks') and the options test memorised statutory facts rather than clinical-reasoning steps; cannot support genuine alternative-selection, AU clinical cut-off, anti-pattern, or multi-step decision-making distinct from a statutory recall question. Better routed to a Psych/Ethics-flavoured discriminator framework if needed.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-month-old ex-term breastfed boy is brought to the GP for intermittent noisy breathing since 4 weeks of age that has slowly become more prominent. The noise is a soft inspiratory stridor that is worse when feeding, crying or lying supine, and almost resolves when held upright or in prone. He is thriving on the 50th centile, has had no fevers, no coryza, no preceding URTI, and feeds well with no choking or coughing. On examination he is alert, T 36.8 deg C, RR 36, HR 130, SpO2 98% on room air, with no recession at rest. Auscultation is clear. There is no barking cough and no hoarse cry. Examination of the oropharynx is normal, the cry is normal in pitch, and there is no haemangioma over the beard distribution. He has not had any feeding-related choking episodes and there is no history suggestive of an inhaled foreign body.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and need for further investigation is correct?

new_options.text: Laryngomalacia; no investigation required if thriving with no red flags (no cyanotic spells, no feeding failure, no failure to thrive), with paediatric ENT referral only if red flags emerge
new_options.rationale: Positional inspiratory stridor starting in the first weeks, worse supine and with feeding and improving prone or upright in a thriving infant without red flags is the classic laryngomalacia picture, the most common cause of infant stridor. Australian practice is observation in the absence of red flags; flexible nasendoscopy and ENT referral are reserved for failure to thrive, severe feeding difficulty, cyanotic spells, or biphasic/atypical stridor, per RCH CPG: Stridor in children.

new_options.text: Subglottic haemangioma; arrange urgent MRI airway given biphasic stridor that worsens supine
new_options.rationale: Subglottic haemangioma typically causes biphasic stridor (not purely inspiratory) and may be associated with a beard-distribution cutaneous haemangioma; not consistent with this presentation.

new_options.text: Viral croup; commence oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg
new_options.rationale: Croup is acute with barking cough, hoarse cry and fever in 6 month to 3 year olds, not chronic positional stridor from the neonatal period.

new_options.text: Inhaled foreign body; arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy
new_options.rationale: A retained foreign body presents acutely with a witnessed choking episode and focal asymmetry on auscultation, not chronic positional stridor.

new_options.text: Vascular ring; arrange CT angiography of the chest given positional stridor
new_options.rationale: Vascular rings cause biphasic stridor often with feeding difficulty and reflux, sometimes worsening with neck extension, and may need cross-sectional imaging; positional improvement in prone is more consistent with laryngomalacia and no concerning features are present.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-week-old ex-39-week infant is brought in by her parents with a 3-day history of coryza, cough and wheeze peaking today. She is fully breastfed but over the last 12 hours has been taking about 40 percent of her usual volume and has had 3 wet nappies in 24 hours (usual 6 to 8). She is alert and pink with T 37.6 deg C, RR 58, HR 162, SpO2 91 percent on room air persistently. There is moderate intercostal and subcostal recession and widespread fine inspiratory crackles plus expiratory wheeze. There have been no apnoeic episodes witnessed by the parents or in the department. She is fully immunised for age. There are no risk factors for severe disease beyond her young age. Capillary refill is 2 seconds.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of features most clearly satisfies RCH admission criteria and dictates immediate disposition?

new_options.text: Respiratory rate of 58 per minute alone; admit for observation
new_options.rationale: An RR of 58 in bronchiolitis is expected and not by itself an admission threshold under RCH CPG; admission depends on SpO2, feeding, apnoea, severe work of breathing or social factors.

new_options.text: Persistent SpO2 91 percent on room air plus feeding under 50 percent of normal; admit for supplemental oxygen and feeding support
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis specifies admission when SpO2 is persistently under 92 percent on room air, feeding is under 50 percent of normal, there is apnoea, severe work of breathing or social concerns. This infant meets both the SpO2 and feeding thresholds; admission is indicated for low-flow oxygen and nasogastric or IV hydration.

new_options.text: Temperature 37.6 deg C with a sibling URTI; admit for sepsis workup
new_options.rationale: Low-grade fever and household viral contact are typical features of bronchiolitis and do not meet admission criteria nor trigger a sepsis workup in a stable child over 6 weeks.

new_options.text: Heart rate 162 with no other abnormality; admit for telemetry
new_options.rationale: Tachycardia of 162 is appropriate for fever and respiratory effort at this age and is not by itself an admission threshold.

new_options.text: Wheeze and crackles without hypoxaemia or feeding compromise; admit for nebulised salbutamol
new_options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; auscultatory findings alone do not meet admission criteria.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-year-old boy with a 2-year diagnosis of asthma on PRN salbutamol presents in winter with worsening wheeze over 6 hours despite three sets of 6 puffs of salbutamol via spacer at home. He has had two ED presentations in the last year and no ICU admissions. On arrival he is too breathless to speak in full sentences, RR 44, HR 142, SpO2 90 percent on room air. There is marked accessory muscle use with tracheal tug and intercostal recession, widespread polyphonic wheeze with prolonged expiratory phase and only moderate air entry bilaterally. He is alert but anxious. PEFR cannot be obtained. He has had no recent oral steroid courses, no fever, and no focal chest signs. Chest examination shows no asymmetry to suggest foreign body or pneumothorax.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate first-line therapy package?

new_options.text: Continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg, ipratropium 250 microgram added to each of the first three nebs, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (maximum 50 mg) as soon as possible, controlled supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 over 94 percent
new_options.rationale: This is severe acute asthma by Australian severity criteria (unable to speak in sentences, SpO2 90 percent, marked work of breathing). National Asthma Council and RCH guidance: continuous or back-to-back nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium added to first three nebs, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg immediately, and titrated oxygen. IV magnesium is the next step if no response.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for one hour; withhold steroids until registrar review
new_options.rationale: MDI-spacer is for mild-moderate disease; severe disease with SpO2 90 percent requires nebulised therapy plus immediate oral steroid.

new_options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes and IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, with no inhaled therapy
new_options.rationale: IV magnesium is for asthma not responding to first-line inhaled bronchodilator plus oral steroid; bypassing inhaled therapy is not standard.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, nebulised salbutamol every 4 hours as required
new_options.rationale: Antibiotics are not routine in acute asthma without focal chest signs or fever, and 4-hourly nebs are inadequate for severe disease.

new_options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg, oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, observe for 4 hours
new_options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for croup; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg is the standard steroid in paediatric acute asthma.

verdicts.new_stem: An 8-year-old with mild persistent asthma attends a routine GP review. His mother reports symptoms remain uncontrolled despite using salbutamol via MDI with a mouthpiece spacer for 12 months. Adherence is good. On observed technique he shakes the canister, attaches it to the spacer, places the mouthpiece in his mouth, fires four puffs into the spacer in rapid succession, then takes two quick breaths without sealing his lips around the mouthpiece between actuations. He has no oromotor or developmental concerns and is growing on the 50th centile. He is asymptomatic with normal spirometry (FEV1 92 percent predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84) at review.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the single most important correction to make to his spacer technique?

new_options.text: Switch from a mouthpiece spacer to a face-mask spacer to maximise drug delivery for his age
new_options.rationale: Face-mask spacers are preferred only when a child cannot reliably seal lips on a mouthpiece, typically under 4 years; at 8 the mouthpiece is preferred when used correctly.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Wash the spacer with hot soapy water before each use and dry it with a clean towel
new_options.rationale: Spacers are washed monthly with detergent and AIR-dried (no towel) to preserve antistatic properties; towel-drying creates static that reduces drug delivery.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Hold the breath for 30 seconds after each inhalation to maximise small-airway deposition
new_options.rationale: Breath-hold of about 5-10 seconds is recommended; 30 seconds offers no additional benefit and is impractical.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Fire one puff at a time, take 4 tidal breaths (or one slow deep breath with 5-second breath-hold) per puff, and maintain a lip seal on the mouthpiece throughout
new_options.rationale: The two most common technique errors are firing multiple puffs without separating them (drug coalesces and impacts on the spacer wall) and breaking the lip seal between breaths. The single most important correction is one puff per actuation with 4 tidal breaths and a maintained lip seal, per the National Asthma Council How to Use Your Spacer factsheet.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Replace the MDI-spacer with a dry-powder inhaler now that he is old enough to follow staged instructions
new_options.rationale: DPIs require a fast inspiratory flow that not all 8-year-olds reliably generate, especially during acute asthma; properly used MDI plus spacer is preferred.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

verdicts.new_stem: A 2-year-old girl presents at 2030 on Friday with 24 hours of a barking cough and hoarse cry that has worsened over the evening. She has had a 2-day coryzal illness. Her father reports increasing work of breathing in the last hour and audible noise on breathing in. In the resuscitation cubicle she is sitting upright on her father's lap, anxious, and refusing to drink. She has continuous inspiratory stridor at rest with marked sternal recession and tracheal tug. There is no drooling, she is afebrile at 37.7 deg C, SpO2 95 percent on room air, RR 36, HR 142. Her cry is hoarse but she can speak in short phrases. She is fully immunised including Hib, with no atopy and no history suggestive of foreign body aspiration. Air entry is symmetrical without focal crackles or wheeze. She has had no previous croup admissions or recurrent stridor between viral illnesses. Weight 13 kg. The clinician calculates a Westley score of 6.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of immediate management and minimum observation?

new_options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg alone, discharge after 1-hour observation if stable
new_options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is appropriate for mild croup (Westley 0-2); a moderate score (3-7) with stridor at rest and marked recession requires nebulised adrenaline plus minimum 4 hours observation for rebound.

new_options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (or prednisolone if no oral access), observe minimum 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose before considering discharge
new_options.rationale: Moderate croup (Westley 3-7) with stridor at rest is treated with nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and oral dexamethasone (0.15-0.6 mg/kg). Adrenaline acts within 30 minutes but lasts about 2 hours; steroid onset is 1-2 hours. The 4-hour minimum observation captures any rebound, per RCH CPG: Croup.

new_options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and urgent ENT for suspected epiglottitis
new_options.rationale: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod posture, toxic appearance and muffled (not hoarse) voice in an unimmunised child; this is typical viral croup.

new_options.text: Heliox via face mask and humidified oxygen; defer dexamethasone pending ENT review
new_options.rationale: Humidified air and heliox have no proven benefit; delaying steroid is harmful because steroid reduces return visits and admission.

new_options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg, ipratropium 250 microgram and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
new_options.rationale: That is acute asthma treatment; beta-agonists do not address subglottic oedema in croup.

verdicts.new_stem: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in the GP clinic 2 weeks after a moderate asthma exacerbation that required oral prednisolone in ED but no admission. She is on fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microgram twice daily and PRN salbutamol via MDI plus spacer. Technique is excellent and adherence is good. Over the past year she has had three exacerbations, each beginning with several days of increased salbutamol use and mild nocturnal cough; on two occasions her mother gave a single dose of salbutamol and waited overnight before review, and the girl ended up needing oral prednisolone. The clinician is now writing a National Asthma Council action plan and wants the family to act earlier. The school has the previous plan but it is 18 months old. The family lives 15 minutes from the nearest ED.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which yellow-zone instruction set is most appropriate to write into her plan?

new_options.text: Continue preventer at current dose; salbutamol 2 puffs every 4 hours; no other change
new_options.rationale: Yellow zone requires intensification; 2 puffs every 4 hours is the same as background PRN dosing and will not catch the early deterioration pattern seen in her past exacerbations.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Self-initiate a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg at home at first sign of worsening before any clinician contact
